title: The ‘Barbie’ Freak-Out, Oscar Nominations Mailbag, and ‘The Zone of Interest’
format: Sean and Amanda open on the Barbie Oscar-snub discourse — an all-time Amanda Hillary Clinton rant included — then run a long nominations mailbag with producer Bobby Wagner, detour into the greatest Best Picture lineups ever, file a Sundance dispatch, and close with a spoiler-heavy talk about The Zone of Interest.

segments.heading: The ‘Barbie’ Freak-Out
segments.note: the snub discourse, after the nominations

films.title: Barbie
films.note: Eight nominations, but Gerwig missed director and Robbie missed actress — the snub that drove the week's discourse. Amanda's No. 2 of the year; she reads it as a Dark Knight-style moment where a beloved movie gets left out and the Academy eventually changes its rules.

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: The comp: a big movie's Best Picture snub that reshaped how the Academy nominates. Poetic, they note, that this is the year Nolan finally gets his makeup call.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: The presumptive front-runner — 'Oppenheimer all the way down,' the way Succession swept TV.

films.title: Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse
films.note: Sean's No. 2 of the year and a movie he had in his own Best Picture slate; an achievement he sees but doesn't respond to the way Amanda responds to Barbie.

films.title: Anatomy of a Fall
films.note: Justine Triet nominated for director — a historic, contemporary, in-your-face film that's become the modern Academy's taste. The Sandra Hüller fancams, Amanda jokes, were all made by her.

films.title: Maestro
films.note: A movie they admire with complicated feelings, now getting dinged harder than it deserves. Sean still rates the molar scene one of the year's best.

films.title: Poor Things
films.note: The kind of nominee people now reserve January and February to catch up on.

segments.heading: The Oscar Nominations Mailbag
segments.note: listener questions, with producer Bobby Wagner

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The last genuinely shocking win — Bong's director upset, then Best Picture. Also the crux of the international-feature question: if it can win both, why does the category still exist?

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: The other memorable surprise, over La La Land — and a small company (A24) that changed Hollywood.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: The behemoth that got upset — the template for a surprise that sticks.

films.title: The Iron Claw
films.note: The most-asked question in the bag: how did it get zero nominations? Sean's answer — a mid-December release, no festival premiere, and A24 betting on Past Lives and Zone of Interest instead. A 'middle movie' that never got the Rain Man path through the system.

films.title: Rain Man
films.note: The middle movie that became a blockbuster because the industry celebrated it — the arc Iron Claw never got to take.

films.title: The Taste of Things
films.note: France picked it over Anatomy of a Fall for international feature on a 4–3 vote — an old-guard, classically composed choice, the subject of a Variety piece Amanda cites.

films.title: American Symphony
films.note: Cited as Obama's 'snub' — one song nomination for the Jon Batiste documentary.

films.title: Babette's Feast
films.note: The reference point for the stately, elegant tradition The Taste of Things belongs to.

films.title: Saltburn
films.note: The Academy-ratio question's exhibit A — Kevin Porter's two-star review comparing 1.37:1 to early-2000s TV shooting widescreen to look important. Also, they argue, boxier framing that plays better on Instagram.

films.title: Asteroid City
films.note: Named in the Academy-ratio-aissance question.

films.title: The Grand Budapest Hotel
films.note: The post-Budapest trend: Academy ratio as a signal a movie is 'not like the other girls.'

films.title: Ida
films.note: Sean's counter-argument — a small, confined film the boxy format actually serves.

films.title: American Honey
films.note: Another case where the ratio fits a smaller, more intimate story.

films.title: Elephant
films.note: Gus Van Sant's school-shooting film — the kind of confined subject that earns the format.

films.title: Nyad
films.note: The Netflix-campaigning question — Annette Bening's nod as evidence of a very good awards team, not of an easier-to-stream advantage.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Dead Reckoning Part One
films.note: The stunts-recognition question — Sean only just learned the franchise had never been nominated, not even in sound. A launchpad for a long tangent about the NFL and NBA rules committees.

films.title: Guardians of the Galaxy
films.note: Amanda's route to a Bradley Cooper Oscar: bring back Rocket Raccoon. Then remembers Gunn is at DC now.

films.title: Beauty and the Beast
films.note: The animated Best Picture precedent, and the kind of undeniable classic that could crack a Big Five category again.

films.title: Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio
films.note: Sean liked it, but not the animated feature that will finally break through to the big categories.

films.title: The Godfather Part II
films.note: Sean's 1975 ceremony (films of 1974) — the run he argues has to come first.

films.title: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
films.note: The 1976 ceremony (films of 1975) — 'this is the one everyone points to.' Sean's teenage proof the Oscars were cool.

films.title: Rocky
films.note: The 1977 ceremony — Rocky over the rest, back when it was only five nominees.

films.title: In the Heat of the Night
films.note: The 1968 ceremony — the year of Pictures at a Revolution.

films.title: Doctor Dolittle
films.note: The 'why not' of the bunch.

films.title: Forrest Gump
films.note: The 1995 ceremony — the 'weak one,' though only in hindsight; Sean's about to do a live Forrest Gump Rewatchables.

films.title: Four Weddings and a Funeral
films.note: Amanda calls it a forever movie; Sean is not so sure.

films.title: Pulp Fiction
films.note: A forever movie, no argument.

films.title: Quiz Show
films.note: The one that's fallen away — a pre-Sorkin 'humans talking in rooms' movie Sean always liked.

films.title: The Shawshank Redemption
films.note: The other forever movie of the year.

films.title: Gone with the Wind
films.note: Winner of 1939, which Sean calls arguably the single best movie year ever — only the 12th time they gave out the awards.

films.title: Dark Victory
films.note: The Bette Davis film — 'an excellent movie.'

films.title: Goodbye, Mr. Chips
films.note: 'Which is okay.'

films.title: Love Affair
films.note: Leo McCarey's 'phenomenal film.'

films.title: Of Mice and Men
films.note: Lewis Milestone's adaptation — 'really good.'

films.title: Stagecoach
films.note: Introduced the world to John Wayne via John Ford.

films.title: Wuthering Heights
films.note: William Wyler's film.

films.title: How Green Was My Valley
films.note: The 1942 ceremony — famously beat Citizen Kane.

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: Nominated, and defeated.

films.title: Suspicion
films.note: Alfred Hitchcock's film.

films.title: The Silence of the Lambs
films.note: The 1992 ceremony — the last big sweep of the top categories.

films.title: Bugsy
films.note: 'Weird movie. I like it.' Sean's Warren Beatty era.

films.title: JFK
films.note: 'Masterful.'

films.title: The Prince of Tides
films.note: Sean's underrated pick — the Pat Conroy novel; Conroy was a family friend who wrote it in their shared Atlanta garage.

films.title: Schindler's List
films.note: The 1994 lineup Sean rates just under the Pulp Fiction year.

films.title: In the Name of the Father
Braveheart
films.note: The 1996 winner — not Sean's favorite year, but Amanda defends Sense and Sensibility as a masterpiece.

films.title: Babe
films.note: 'Which everyone loves.'

films.title: Il Postino
films.note: 'Not so much.'

films.title: Sense and Sensibility
films.note: Amanda's hill to die on.

films.title: No Country for Old Men
films.note: The 2008 lineup — 'very, very good.'

films.title: Atonement
films.note: Amanda loves it; Sean thinks it a noble effort at an impossible adaptation.

films.title: Juno
films.note: Sean's holdout — insists it doesn't hold up; Bobby, born-adjacent to its release, disagrees.

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: The 2018 winner — 'a great year.'

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: Bobby's grievance — glossed over in the 2019 talk despite Sean's two hours in James Mangold's office.

films.title: Jojo Rabbit
films.note: The dent in an otherwise strong 2019.

films.title: Joker
films.note: Sean likes it, but isn't sure it had to be a Best Picture nominee.

segments.heading: The Debut, Breakthrough, and Swap Ballots
segments.note: hypothetical categories, and one acting nominee swapped per category

films.title: Past Lives
films.note: Both hosts' No. 1 debut — Celine Song. Also the source of a breakthrough (Teo Yoo) and a should-have-been nominee (Greta Lee).

films.title: Talk to Me
films.note: The Philippou brothers on Amanda's debut ballot.

films.title: They Cloned Tyrone
films.note: Juel Taylor — Amanda's 'good one.'

films.title: Rye Lane
films.note: Raine Allen-Miller — on both debut ballots.

films.title: Creed III
films.note: Michael B. Jordan's directing debut — released too early and, after the Jonathan Majors story, near memory-holed; Sean thought Jordan a better director than performer here.

films.title: American Fiction
films.note: Cord Jefferson's debut — five nominations, and a face Sean would love to see at the podium.

films.title: Earth Mama
films.note: Savannah Leaf — on the long list.

films.title: Fair Play
films.note: Chloe Domont's debut.

films.title: Bottoms
films.note: Marshawn Lynch as a breakthrough.

films.title: Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret.
films.note: Abby Ryder Fortson as a breakthrough.

films.title: Pacific Rim Uprising
films.note: Dredged up to test whether Cailee Spaeny counts as a 'debut' — she doesn't break through here.

films.title: Pacific Rim
films.note: The original, for the record.

films.title: A Thousand and One
films.note: Amanda's swap: Teyana Taylor in for Annette Bening — one of her favorite movies of the year.

films.title: Blackberry
films.note: Glenn Howerton for supporting actor — a bigness both hosts would take over Mark Ruffalo in Poor Things.

films.title: All of Us Strangers
films.note: Amanda's supporting swap — Jamie Bell in Ruffalo's place.

films.title: Rustin
films.note: The Colman Domingo debate — a by-the-numbers biopic role, though his nomination is historic; Sean would swap Andrew Scott in.

films.title: May December
films.note: Sean's supporting-actress swap — Rachel McAdams over Emily Blunt; and a shame about what happened to the film's awards run.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: Lily Gladstone's actress campaign hangs over the swap talk; Sean would put Julianne Moore in over America Ferrera.

films.title: The Devil Wears Prada
films.note: What Emily Blunt should have been nominated for, per Amanda.

films.title: Mary Poppins Returns
films.note: Blunt was 'actually very good in that,' they decide.

films.title: Presence
films.note: Steven Soderbergh's ghost story told from the perspective of the spectral being — sold to Neon. Sean will watch anything Soderbergh releases.

films.title: I Saw the TV Glow
films.note: Jane Schoenbrunn's follow-up, about trans identity and the culture we consume — seemingly the most acclaimed film of the festival.

films.title: We're All Going to the World's Fair
films.note: Schoenbrunn's earlier film, a huge hit, the reason to be excited for the new one.

films.title: The Outrun
films.note: Saoirse Ronan; pundits telling people to 'lock in' Best Actress on January 25th, which Sean finds funny.

films.title: Will & Harper
films.note: The Will Ferrell documentary — a cross-country trip with his friend Harper, who came out as trans. A long, loud standing ovation.

films.title: Super/Man: The Christopher Reeve Story
films.note: Unacquired, but 'what it says on the label' — a Ringer colleague found it powerful.

films.title: In a Violent Nature
films.note: Sean's favorite — slow-cinema horror following a resurrected slasher through the woods, with amazing kills. He watched it Sunday at noon, crushing a bag of pretzels. Likely a Shudder release.

films.title: Devo
films.note: Chris Smith's documentary on the band.

films.title: Girls State
films.note: The inversion of Boys State — young women building a mock government.

films.title: Boys State
films.note: The Jesse Moss and Amanda McBaine documentary this one answers to.

films.title: The Remarkable Life of Ibelin
films.note: Benjamin Ree's documentary about a Dutch boy with a degenerative disease who lived a full second life inside World of Warcraft — reconstructed from the game's chat logs. Sean: 'I've never seen anything like this.' Netflix, later this year.

films.title: Good One
films.note: India Donaldson's film — a 17-year-old on an annual hike with her father and his best friend; all very normal until a quiet, powerful turn.

films.title: A Real Pain
films.note: Jesse Eisenberg's second directorial feature — two cousins (Kieran Culkin, Eisenberg) on a heritage trip to Poland. Sean pitches it as a strange double feature with The Zone of Interest.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: Jonathan Glazer's Auschwitz commandant film, from the Martin Amis novel, with Christian Friedel and Sandra Hüller — a Big Brother-style surveillance grid of the Höss family's happy home, the horror always just over the wall in sound and smoke. Sean's Parasite-style inflection point for what the Academy is now; both agree it wins international feature and should win sound. A hard movie to love, astonishing to watch.

films.title: Birth
films.note: The Glazer film Sean and Amanda light up over — 'so, so good' — hoping his rumored next project lives up to it.

films.title: Killers of the Flower Moon
films.note: The coda comparison — Scorsese tries to portray the horror until he can't; Glazer doesn't even try, cutting instead to present-day Auschwitz as a cleaned, glass-cased museum.

films.title: A Real Pain
films.note: The unexpected match — Sean had just seen its cage of shoes at Sundance, the same museumified image Zone ends on.

films.title: Seabiscuit
films.note: The warning to newcomers dutifully checking off a nominee: 'This is not Seabiscuit.'
The anti-comp — 'there's no thrill ride to this devastation'; the film is fixed cameras and dread, not a war spectacle.
